parts.answer: INPUT Item_Quantity[Order_Item] Order_Item ← Order_Item + 1 ELSE Order_Complete ← TRUE Order_Number ← Order_Number + 1 ENDIF UNTIL Order_Complete = TRUE OUTPUT "Order Number ", Order_Number Counter ← WHILE Item_Code[Counter] <> "X" DO Menu_Count ← Menu_Place[Counter] OUTPUT Item_Code[Counter], " ", Menu_Item{Menu_Count], " ", Price[Menu_Count], " ",Item_Quantity[Counter] Counter ← Counter + 1 ENDWHILE OUTPUT "Total cost of order = ", Total_Cost Daily_Order_Code[Daily_Order_Counter] ← Order_Number Daily_Total_Cost_of_Order[Daily_Order_Counter] ← Total_Cost four from: • Explanation of using an input prompt and statement to enter the percent of the takings that are profit • Explanation of how the total takings are calculated • Explanation of how the percentage profits are calculated (from the input value) • Explanation of how the output is done // Explanation showing correct output statement with the daily takings, profit and percent value used in the calculation 0478/21 October/November 2018

1 mark for each correct test data item and related reason for Task 3 which requires a percentage to be input (Answers MUST relate to pre-release task) e.g. Test data: -10 Reason: To check that negative values of percentage are rejected (or can identify a loss) Test data: 7.5 Reason: To check that normal data is accepted Test data: 7Percent Reason: To check that incorrect data / data types are rejected 0478/21 October/November 2018 Section B 1 mark for each correct line, maximum 5 marks Term Description Top-down design Structure diagram Flowchart Pseudocode Library routine Trace table Breaks down a system into successively smaller pieces. Shows the hierarchy of the different components which make up a system. Shows the steps representing an algorithm using various shapes of boxes. Describes a program using a simplified high-level notation. Pre-written code to include in your own program to carry out a common task. Shows the values of variables as you manually test your program. 0478/21 October/November 2018 1 mark for description 1 mark for example, e.g. To test if the value input falls between a given upper bound and a given lower bound « If a month has to be input using an integer, it must be between 1 and 12 inclusive. 1 mark for description 1 mark for example, e.g. To test if the data input is over/under a certain number of characters « An international telephone number can be no longer than 15 digits. 1 mark for description 1 mark for example, e.g. To test if the input is of the correct data type « If the input is expecting integer(s) to be entered, it will not permit a string to be entered.
1 mark for each point: • Expects 50 numbers to be input • Totals the numbers as they are entered / carries out a running total • Outputs the result after the numbers have all been entered
Total ← Count ← REPEAT INPUT Num Total ← Total + Num Count ← Count + 1 UNTIL Count = 50 OUTPUT Total

1 mark for each correct point in description, e.g. • Use a variable for the counter upper limit « • « that is input by the user. or • loop using a condition control « • « until condition is met Accept alternative correct ranges e.g. Count ← UNTIL Count > 50 0478/21 October/November 2018
